thread_tweets.text:
1. Revenue > Narrative

VCs explicitly said the "easy financing era is over." Projects need real users and revenue. Token models as exit strategies are heavily discounted.
thread_tweets.full_text:
1. Revenue > Narrative

VCs explicitly said the "easy financing era is over." Projects need real users and revenue. Token models as exit strategies are heavily discounted.

thread_tweets.text:
2. The TradFi convergence

The line between crypto VC and traditional VC is blurring fast. Stablecoins, RWAs, and institutional DeFi are the bridge.
thread_tweets.full_text:
2. The TradFi convergence

The line between crypto VC and traditional VC is blurring fast. Stablecoins, RWAs, and institutional DeFi are the bridge.

thread_tweets.text:
3. AI is the new demand driver

AI agents are creating net new demand for crypto rails (payments, compute, identity), not just repackaging existing narratives.
thread_tweets.full_text:
3. AI is the new demand driver

AI agents are creating net new demand for crypto rails (payments, compute, identity), not just repackaging existing narratives.

thread_tweets.text:

The CLARITY Act, GENIUS Act, and MiCA are unlocking institutional capital. VCs are front-running the regulatory green light.

tweets.full_text:
No matter what you build in Web3, the playbook has 5 rules ↓

1. Revenue > Narrative

1. Revenue > Narrative

VCs explicitly said the "easy financing era is over." Projects need real users and revenue. Token models as exit strategies are heavily discounted.

2. The TradFi convergence

The line between crypto VC and traditional VC is blurring fast. Stablecoins, RWAs, and institutional DeFi are the bridge.

3. AI is the new demand driver

AI agents are creating net new demand for crypto rails (payments, compute, identity), not just repackaging existing narratives.

4. Regulatory clarity as catalyst

The CLARITY Act, GENIUS Act, and MiCA are unlocking institutional capital. VCs are front-running the regulatory green light.

5. Asia as the battleground

Multiple funds (Kaia-Simsan, AppWorks, OKX Ventures) are specifically targeting Southeast Asia and Korea as the next stablecoin adoption frontier.

tweets.text:
I recently read a beautiful quote: Humans are not statues, they’re rivers.

There’s nothing permanent about us. We’re constantly changing.

Markets are the same.

Investors spend an enormous amount of time comparing the present to the past, looking for patterns and analogies. But the current situation is never the same as a past situation. It may rhyme, but it is always different in important ways.

Every crisis has different participants, incentives, valuations, technologies, regulations, and narratives.

Like a river, the market is never the same market twice.

The current S&P 500 is dominated by technology and AI-related companies. Twenty years ago it was dominated by different industries and different narratives. Twenty years from now it will likely be dominated by companies we barely think about today.

All these charts are worthless. They’re just pictures on a wall showing how things changed, offering precisely zero investment value.

Since the dawn of time, the only two market forces that have truly repeated themselves are fear and greed.

That’s why I pay attention to the $VIX . Everything else is mostly noise.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
The dividend yield for the S&P 500 recently fell below 1%, a level it hasn't seen in more than 20 years.

$SPY https://t.co/mCzvcrD6h8

tweets.text:
The credit system functions like a circulatory system that delivers nutrients and buying power to various parts of the economy. A system is considered healthy when borrowed money is used to generate productivity, which in turn produces income. However, it becomes unhealthy when debts and debt service costs increase relative to income. This debt service acts like plaque in the circulatory system, squeezing out spending. Because one individual's debt represents another individual's asset, with holders expecting a good return, increased selling leads to supply and demand issues. When combined with high debt service, it ultimately impairs the system's mechanics.
@edels0n
tweets.full_text:

Because one individual's debt represents another individual's asset, with holders expecting a good return, increased selling leads to supply and demand issues. When combined with high debt service, it ultimately impairs the system's mechanics.

tweets.text:
This was always possible anyway by just sending your transactions to a specific validator. Exchanges did this historically with miners to dampen fee volatility. I wouldn’t be surprised they have similar deals in place. 

It only gets problematic if validators also chose not to build in blocks from non-consortium validators.

thread_tweets.text: @devanshmehta @ethconf @VladZamfir Haha that is indeed a blast from the past. I don’t know PoS in Ethereum today does anything to stop it. DAG / Ghost is probably starting point as it helps provide evidence that a block is missing. I can’t really recall of any academic papers that tackle it properly.
thread_tweets.full_text: @devanshmehta @ethconf @VladZamfir Haha that is indeed a blast from the past. I don’t know PoS in Ethereum today does anything to stop it. DAG / Ghost is probably starting point as it helps provide evidence that a block is missing. I can’t really recall of any academic papers that tackle it properly.

thread_tweets.text: Actually maybe worth me rephrasing. The threat models of a consensus protocol generally deals with this action, but I don’t recall an economic incentive scheme that attempts to punish it. The only thing is PoS Ethereum’s inactivity leak, so validators would eventually have to be included I think? Might depend on consortium size.
thread_tweets.full_text: Actually maybe worth me rephrasing. The threat models of a consensus protocol generally deals with this action, but I don’t recall an economic incentive scheme that attempts to punish it. The only thing is PoS Ethereum’s inactivity leak, so validators would eventually have to be included I think? Might depend on consortium size.

tweets.full_text:
This was always possible anyway by just sending your transactions to a specific validator. Exchanges did this historically with miners to dampen fee volatility. I wouldn’t be surprised they have similar deals in place. 

It only gets problematic if validators also chose not to build in blocks from non-consortium validators.

@devanshmehta @ethconf @VladZamfir Haha that is indeed a blast from the past. I don’t know PoS in Ethereum today does anything to stop it. DAG / Ghost is probably starting point as it helps provide evidence that a block is missing. I can’t really recall of any academic papers that tackle it properly.

Actually maybe worth me rephrasing. The threat models of a consensus protocol generally deals with this action, but I don’t recall an economic incentive scheme that attempts to punish it. The only thing is PoS Ethereum’s inactivity leak, so validators would eventually have to be included I think? Might depend on consortium size.
